data.title: Nomura Tax-Free USA Intermediate Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The US municipal bond market recorded positive returns for 4Q25, driven primarily by strong performance in October. The US economy showed signs of slowing, as it had in previous quarters, resulting in monetary easing. Additionally, political uncertainty and fiscal policy influenced the market. The municipal market contended with a technical headwind of heavy issuance, which was a theme all year. Demand was strong, however, as municipal mutual funds experienced inflows. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 1.56%. The intermediate segment of the yield curve outperformed while the investment grade segment outperformed high yield segments.

Increased trade tensions between the US and China, giving way to a truce, marked the beginning of the last quarter of 2025. Economic indicators continued to show resilience with 3Q gross domestic product ('GDP') growth tracking close to 3.5%, maintaining the pace of 2Q (based on the Atlanta Federal Reserve's GDPNow forecast). Government fiscal deficits and artificial intelligence ('AI') spending remained tailwinds. Investors mostly shrugged off the US government shutdown during the quarter, viewing it as unlikely to significantly impact growth. The lack of important data releases was the larger concern. Labor markets showed some softness with the unemployment rate rising to 4.6%, while inflation appears to be moderating with November's Consumer Price Index ('CPI') reported at 2.7% versus an expected 3.1%. However, the impact of the government shutdown on data quality was visible with some parts of the CPI report in particular reflecting missing October data. The reported disinflation, while welcome, will need to hold up in coming months before the US Federal Reserve (Fed) and investors are convinced that further easing is likely.

The economy and the markets were also supported by policy stimulus – deficit spending and Fed easing. The fiscal impulse is widely expected to continue in 2026 with tax refunds under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act ('OBBBA') flowing through in the first quarter. The Fed resumed its easing in September, but cracks are visible within the committee with some members concerned about the downside risks to labor markets while others fret about stimulating even higher inflation. While less-restrictive monetary policy as a risk-management exercise appeared to be a reasonable compromise, investors are increasingly worried that further easing may signal a weakening of the Fed's resolve on inflation.

The Treasury yield curve steepened as yields on the front end of the curve fell while rising on the long segment during the quarter. For example, the 2-year Treasury yield fell 14 basis points (bps), while the 10-year spot remained unchanged and the 30-year segment rose 9 bps. (One basis point equals one-hundredth of a percentage point.) Municipal yields rose on the front end, fell in the intermediate segments, and remained unchanged on the long end. The AAA municipal 2-year yield rose 9 bps, the 10-year fell 16 bps, and the 30-year was flat. Consequently, municipal bonds underperformed Treasury bonds on the front end while outperforming in the intermediate and long segments of the curve. MMD AAA/US Treasury tightened roughly 170-390 bps in the 10- and 30-year segments of the curve. Despite the strong ratio movement, the 10-year tranche ended the year at roughly the same level that it started, and the 30-year tranche was wider than where it began the year. The municipal curve, as measured by the AAA 2-year minus the AAA 30-year, flattened 9 bps for the quarter but still was 77 bps steeper for the full year. It finished at 185 bps after being as steep as 243 bps in August.

Municipal mutual funds recorded inflows of $19.5 billion for the quarter. Open-end funds accounted for $6.6 billion while exchange-traded funds (ETFs) registered $12.9 billion in inflows. Investment grade funds took in roughly 88% of the inflows. From a duration perspective, long-term (10+ years) funds took in roughly $8.3 billion. More specifically, long ETFs took in most of that amount. In the last few weeks of 2025, year-end tax-loss swapping from open-end funds into ETFs occurred. For example, open-end funds experienced roughly -$1.3 billion in outflows while ETFs took in roughly $2.8 billion in inflows during the last three weeks of the year. For the full calendar year, municipal mutual funds experienced $51.0 billion in inflows with ETFs accounting for roughly 67% of that amount.

Issuance for the quarter was robust, a continual trend all year as issuers attempted to lock in borrowing costs amid fiscal policy uncertainty. More than $144 billion in new-issue supply came to the market, 15% higher than 4Q24. There were more than a dozen deals over $1.0 billion in size. For the full calendar year, the municipal bond market issuance registered a record, totaling more than $590 billion. As stated previously, the uptick could be attributed to a “pull forward” effect in preparation for potential fiscal policy limitations as well as the suppressed need for capital and the impact of inflation on the market’s funding needs.

Against this backdrop, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 1.56% for the quarter, bolstered by strong performance in the first month. The index’s October return of 1.24% was the first positive October return in the past six years and well above the 20-year monthly average of -0.31%. The index returned 4.25% for the full year, above the 20-year annual average of 3.60%. The 10-year segment of the curve was the strongest performer while the long end (20+ years) was the weakest for the year, an aberration from long-term curve trends.

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index outperformed other major US bond benchmarks for 4Q25 (see returns table below). The strongest-performing part of the municipal curve was the 15-year (12-17 years), which returned 2.84%. The 3-year segment (2-4 years) was the weakest, returning 0.46%. The strongest-performing investment grade credit tranche was BBB-rated bonds, returning 1.77%, while the A-rated segment was the weakest, returning 1.50%. The Bloomberg High-Yield Municipal Bond Index returned 1.15% for the quarter, modestly trailing the Bloomberg US Corporate High-Yield Index’s 1.31% gain. Similar to the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, the Bloomberg High Yield Index’s 12-17 year segment of the yield curve outperformed. High yield tobacco and high yield Puerto Rico bonds, which together make up roughly 21% of the Bloomberg High-Yield Municipal Bond Index, returned -1.75% and 1.12%, respectively.

Market statistics

During 4Q25, US Treasury yields fell by 4 bps in the 5-year segment, remained flat in the 10-year, and rose 9 bps in the 30-year. The municipal bond yield rose 9 bps in the 5-year segment, fell 16 bps in the 10-year, and remained flat in the 30-year.

As shown in the following table, MMD AAA/US Treasury ratios were mixed with outperformance on the front end and underperformance on the long end for the quarter.

The municipal yield curve, as measured by the spread between 2-year and 30-year MMD AAA-rated bond yields, flattened by 9 bps, to 185 bps. Two-year high-grade tax-exempt bond yields rose by 9 bps, while 30-year high-grade tax-exempt bond yields were unchanged. (Data: Thomson Reuters.)

BBB-rated securities led the investment grade municipal market during 4Q25, returning 1.77%. Credit spreads, as measured by MMD yields on 30-year BBB-rated general obligation bonds minus 30-year AAA-rated general obligation bonds, were 3 bps tighter. At the end of the quarter, the spread was 87 bps.

Municipal bond total returns by rating for the quarter were as follows:

The housing sector returned 1.77% and was the strongest sector within the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index. The weakest sector was industrial redevelopment/pollution control revenue (IDR/PCR) bonds, which returned 0.65%.

Municipal supply was approximately $144.0 billion for 4Q25, a 15% increase relative to 4Q24. (Sources: Bloomberg Finance LP, J.P. Morgan.)

Within the Fund

The Fund's full allocation to out-of-benchmark below investment grade bonds detracted from performance, returning -1.68% compared to the benchmark return of 1.48% and the Bloomberg High Yield Municipal Bond Index return of 1.15%. From a curve perspective, the Fund's roughly 45.0% exposure to out-of-benchmark long bonds (17+ years) detracted. These bonds returned 1.26% compared to the benchmark return of 1.48%. For reference, the 17+ year allocation within the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 1.85%.

The 15-year (12-17 years) maturity segment contributed for the quarter. This was the strongest-performing maturity segment in the benchmark, returning 2.82%. The Fund was market weight and outperformed in this part of the curve, returning 2.90%.

Outlook

The municipal market delivered strong returns in 2025. The short/intermediate portion of the curve drove performance while the long end lagged, an aberration from long-term norms. However, the municipal curve did begin to normalize toward the back half of the year. For example, the MMD AAA 2-year/30-year curve was as steep as 242 bps but finished the year at 185 bps. We believe the curve can continue to flatten as the 10-year average is 130 bps.

After record issuance in 2025, we believe next year's new issue volume will be similarly elevated with sufficient demand to offset the issuance. Fund flows were positive in 2025. These flows were primarily directed toward long (10+ year) ETFs and intermediate (5-10 year) funds, while long-term (10+ year) open end funds have yet to receive significant inflows. We believe there is still significant money parked in short-term vehicles waiting to be deployed. If the Fed continues its easing program, we think the inflow momentum should continue. In addition to organic fund flows, calls and coupon interest, which hit record highs in 2025, are projected to be even higher in 2026 and should be a supportive technical.

As we noted in previous quarters, we believe rates will be range-bound but ultimately lower by the end of 2026. However, we expect periods of volatility, which we would view as an opportunity to add to allocations. In our view, municipal credit remains fundamentally strong, bolstered by solid economic performance. Additionally, we generally do not anticipate a significant degradation in municipal credit. Yields in the municipal market offer investors the opportunity to receive healthy tax-free income, accompanied by attractive total return potential. We recommend investors consider using volatility to add to their municipal exposures.

Market risk is the risk that all or a majority of the securities in a certain market – like the stock market or bond market – will decline in value because of factors such as adverse political or economic conditions, future expectations, investor confidence, or heavy institutional selling. • Fixed income securities can lose value, including the possible loss of principal. The Bloomberg 3-15 Year Blend Municipal Bond Index measures the total return performance of investment grade, US tax-exempt bonds with maturities from 2 to 17 years. The Lipper Intermediate Municipal Debt Funds Average compares funds that invest in municipal debt issues with dollar-weighted average maturities of 5 to 10 years. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index measures the total return performance of the long-term investment grade tax-exempt bond market. The Bloomberg High-Yield Municipal Bond Index measures the total return performance of the long-term, non-investment-grade tax-exempt bond market. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment grade, US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. The Bloomberg US Treasury Index measures the performance of US Treasury bonds and notes that have at least one year to maturity. The Bloomberg US Corporate High-Yield Index is composed of US dollar-denominated, non-investment-grade corporate bonds for which the middle rating among Moody's Investors Service, Inc., Fitch, Inc., and Standard & Poor's is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below. The Dow Jones Industrial Average is an often-quoted market indicator that comprises 30 widely held US blue-chip stocks. The S&P 500 Index measures the performance of 500 mostly large-cap stocks weighted by market value and is often used to represent performance of the US stock market. The US Consumer Price Index ('CPI') is a measure of inflation that is calculated by the US Department of Labor, representing changes in prices of all goods and services purchased for consumption by urban households.

The yield curve is a line that plots the interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality, but differing maturity dates. The most frequently reported yield curve compares the 3-month, 2-year, 5-year, and 30-year US Treasury debt. This yield curve is used as a benchmark for other debt in the market, such as mortgage rates or bank lending rates. It is also used to predict changes in economic output and growth. The shape of the yield curve is closely scrutinized because it helps to give an idea of future interest rate change and economic activity. There are three main types of yield curve shapes: normal, inverted and flat (or humped). A normal yield curve is one in which longer maturity bonds have a higher yield compared to shorter-term bonds due to the risks associated with time. An inverted yield curve is one in which the shorter-term yields are higher than the longer-term yields, which can be a sign of upcoming recession. A flat (or humped) yield curve is one in which the shorter- and longer-term yields are very close to each other, which is also a predictor of an economic transition. The slope of the yield curve is also seen as important: the greater the slope, the greater the gap between short- and long-term rates. Gross domestic product is a measure of all goods and services produced by a nation in a year. It is a measure of economic activity.

data.title: Gulfport Energy: Projected To Generate Over $500 Million In 2026 Free Cash Flow
data.description:
Gulfport Energy (GPOR) met expectations in Q4 2025 and provided 2026 guidance calling for flat total production growth and 4% liquids production growth.

At current 2026 strip prices, Gulfport is projected to generate $510 million in free cash flow, much of which is expected to go towards share repurchases. Gulfport was targeting $140 million in Q1 2026 share repurchases alone.

I think that Gulfport is getting a bit overvalued though, at least based on my long-term commodity prices of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. Gulfport's enterprise value (at $210 per share) is approaching 1.1x proved PV-10 at those commodity prices.

My fair valuation range for Gulfport at those commodity prices (after 2026) is $172 to $188 per share. This is a $5 increase from my previous estimate, due to improved 2026 commodity prices.

Q4 2025 Results

Gulfport averaged 1,097.4 MMCFE (10% liquids) per day in total production in Q4 2025, which was a 2% decrease compared to its Q3 2025 production. Gulfport's liquids production decreased considerably by 17% quarter-over-quarter, with oil production down 31% and its production of NGLs down by 11%.

Gulfport's capex budget was frontloaded into the first half of the year, contributing to its production tailing off later in 2025. Gulfport's liquids production is also significantly affected by which areas (such as Utica condensate vs. Utica dry gas) the wells it brings online are from.

Gulfport's full year 2025 production ended up at 1.039 Bcfe per day, which was roughly in-line with its guidance for 1.04 Bcfe per day. Gulfport originally expected 1.04 to 1.065 Bcfe per day in 2025 production, but then revised it to the low end of that range due to the impact of unplanned midstream outages and constraints.

Gulfport's 2025 total production declined 1% year-over-year, although its liquids production increased by nearly 30% year-over-year.

2026 Production Expectations

Gulfport expects to average around 1.0425 Bcfe per day (11% liquids) in 2026 production at guidance midpoint. This would be roughly flat total production growth and 4% liquids production growth compared to Gulfport's 2025 production. Gulfport's 2026 guidance includes the impact of winter storm downtime that modestly reduced its full year production expectations.

Gulfport expects its production to bottom out at under 1 Bcfe per day in Q2 2026 before increasing again in the second half of 2026. Gulfport's guidance calls for a 5% increase in year-over-year total production in Q4 2026, with its liquids percentage going from 10% in Q4 2025 to around 12% in Q4 2026.

2026 Outlook

At the 1.0425 Bcfe per day midpoint of its 2026 guidance, Gulfport is projected to generate $1.514 billion in 2026 revenues after hedges.

This is based on current strip prices of $69 WTI oil and $3.90 NYMEX natural gas.

Gulfport is hedged on roughly 54% of its 2026 natural gas production with a mix of swaps and collars and is less hedged on oil and NGLs.

Type Units $/Unit $ Million
Natural Gas [MCF] 337,807,500 $3.68 $1,243
NGLs (Barrels) 5,088,100 $31.00 $158
Oil (Barrels) 2,029,400 $62.50 $127
Hedge Value -$14
Total Revenue $1,514

Gulfport's capex budget for 2026 is approximately $415 million, excluding discretionary acreage acquisitions. This budget includes $365 million to $390 million in operated D&C capex as well as $35 million to $40 million for maintenance land and seismic capex.

That budget also includes $10 million to initiate activity in its Marcellus North position.

Total Expenses

This results in a projection of $510 million in free cash flow for Gulfport in 2026 at current strip prices. This doesn't include the impact of cash income taxes, although Gulfport previously mentioned that it expected cash income taxes to be under 5% of its free cash flow in 2026 and 2027.

Gulfport had around $37 million remaining at the end of 2025 out of its previously announced $100 million program for discretionary acreage acquisitions (mostly in Belmont and Monroe Counties). It may continue with discretionary acreage acquisitions after that program wraps up in Q1 2026.

That leaves plenty of funds for continued share repurchases (as well as potential debt reduction). Gulfport mentioned that it was aiming to allocate more than $140 million towards share repurchases in Q1 2026 alone, including some repurchases from Silver Point Capital.

Notes On Valuation

I am increasing my estimate of Gulfport's value by $5 per share, to an updated range of $172 to $188 per share. This is based on current 2026 strip prices and then $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time.

Gulfport's Q4 2025 performance and 2026 guidance was in-line with my expectations, so the increase in its estimated value is essentially due to improved free cash flow projections with higher 2026 commodity prices. When I previously looked at Gulfport, the 2026 oil strip was much lower (in the mid-$50s), while the natural gas strip prices were also around $0.15 to $0.20 lower than current strip prices.

At the midpoint of $180 per share, this translates into an enterprise value for Gulfport that is approximately 0.95x proved PV-10 and around 1.55x PDP PV-10 at my long-term commodity prices of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. This enterprise valuation calculation is based on Gulfport's 18.8 million outstanding common shares and $0.8 billion in net debt as of the end of 2025.

Conclusion

Gulfport is projected to generate $510 million in free cash flow in 2026 at current strip prices while slightly increasing liquids production (and keeping total production flat) compared to 2025. It intends to put much of that free cash flow to continued share repurchases.

At close to $210 per share though, I believe that Gulfport is getting a bit overvalued. Gulfport's enterprise value at that share price is approaching 1.1x proved PV-10 (and around 1.75x PDP PV-10) at $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Thus Gulfport's upside from $210 would significantly depend on higher natural gas prices than that $3.75 longer-term assumption of mine. I am maintaining a neutral outlook for Gulfport for now, although it is close to a downgrade based on valuation concerns.

data.title: Equinor: Trading The Iran War Effect Should Not Distract From The Positive Fundamentals
data.description:
Investment thesis.

Equinor (EQNR) continues to show a great deal of resilience in the face of what was until recently an environment of weak oil prices, as well as European natural gas prices seemingly no longer responding to the usual price signals, such as low storage levels. The latter is a particularly important fundamental factor for the company, since Equinor is by far Europe's largest natural gas supplier.

The Iran conflict-induced oil & gas price rally, which will likely endure for the duration of the conflict, is a classic investment opportunity to sell high on a non-fundamentals-related event and then buy back shares at a significantly lower price once the event subsides. While I do intend to take profits on my Equinor position, because it is better-positioned to outperform its peers during this crisis, I intend to take profits on my other oil-related positions first, before I start reducing my Equinor position. When the crisis subsides, assuming that it will, leading to Equinor's share price to decline, I intend to replenish my position.

Maintaining a buy position on surging energy prices.

The last time I covered Equinor was in late December, and its share price was $23, versus about $31/share as I write this.

The rise in its share price since the last time I covered this stock did not come with a proportional rise in its dividend, it is therefore less attractive in this regard, especially once Norway's dividend tax law is accounted for. It is also trading at a significantly higher P/E ratio, since the rise in its stock price was not driven by higher earnings. At the same time, my outlook for the European natural gas market, which has a high impact on Equinor's financial performance prospects has become more bullish. Inventory levels are low and with the Iran crisis disrupting LNG exports from Qatar, and oil prices also rising, Equinor's share price should rise going forward, for the duration of the conflict, and perhaps even beyond, depending on how the war ends.

Equinor's financial results demonstrate resilience amid weak external factors.

Equinor saw a decline in revenues and profits in 2025, as expected, given unfavorable oil & gas prices in 2025, relative to the prior year. Net income declined by 43% in 2025, relative to 2024, coming in at $5.06 billion. A 13% drop in the price of liquids sold was a major factor in the sharp decline in profits. Revenues came in at $106.5 billion, which was a 3% increase compared with 2024. A 3% increase in its total upstream output as well as higher electricity output, were key drivers of higher revenues, despite the lower oil & gas price environment.

It should be noted that Equinor's renewables segment continues to produce losses. There was an improvement, with adjusted operating losses coming in at $214 million in 2025, down from $375 million in 2024.

The outlook for Equinor's company-specific performance this year is solid, with continued upstream growth and share buybacks, combined with its generous dividend, providing a robust investor reward mechanism.

Short & Long-term effects on Equinor due to the Iran war.

The latest news on the Iran war is less than encouraging for the broader market outlook. It looks like the Strait of Hormuz is set to be closed to naval traffic for the duration of this war. At the same time, it is hard to see an off-ramp for either side. Based on the initial targeting against Iran's leadership, as well as oficial coments, it seems that the bet was on regime change. As of right now, it does not seem to have had that effect. Iran, on the other hand, is betting on the closing of oil shipping through the strait as a means to put pressure on the global economy.

Oil has been the most immediate concern in the media; however, Qatar's announcement that it is suspending production & shipments of LNG has a far more immediate & profound impact on Equinor, as Europe's largest natural gas provider.

The spike in Europe's natural gas spot price of roughly 60% so far this week, may not seem like much more than an insignificant blip on the five-year chart. The reason for it is that the 2022 price spike was so severe that the recent spike barely registers. If Qatar's LNG shipments continue to be shut in for a prolonged period, the minor european TTF spot price blip has the potential to develop into a repeat of the 2022 European gas price crisis.

As the chart above shows, Europe's natural gas inventories already resemble the 2022 situation. The market's reasoning behind prices not reacting to the situation has been the thesis that inventory levels matter far less, given the large volumes of LNG on the global market, which provide far more supply flexibility.

Qatar is officially only the third-largest exporter of LNG, which on the surface suggests that the loss of its exports is not terribly impactful.

In reality, Qatar is one of the three main pillars of the global LNG supply chain, with Russia being a distant fourth. Losing any of the three pillars has a massive impact on global natural gas markets.

Most of Qatar's LNG exports are sent to Asia, not Europe. The problem is that countries like Japan & S. Korea are left with no alternative but to bid up the price of LNG in order to capture more of America's exports, which are currently predominantly headed to Europe. Even though winter seems to be over, the low inventory levels that Europe is entering this crisis with make it imperative to continue securing most of the US LNG shipments available on the market in order to build up an adequate storage cushion for next winter, which is why it will have no choice but to respond to Asian buyers bidding up the price.

A summary of the global oil market situation.

It is not entirely clear whether oil tankers are still getting through the Strait of Hormuz; it seems that even if any vessels are getting through, the flow is minimal, and it comes at a high risk, given that some ships were already targeted.

Even if a small fraction of the roughly 20 mb/d of oil that usually flows through that spot is still getting through, a prolonged disruption affecting most of that oil flow can have a massively bullish impact on the global oil market. The situation could result in a drawdown in global inventories at a rate of about 1 billion barrels every 50 days. The price of oil will probably start rising at a much more robust rate than it is currently if the disruption in shipments lasts beyond the point when we will start seeing the supply shortfall show up in inventory data from various markets.

America is nearly self-sufficient when it comes to crude oil supplies, with the potential to source more from other major exporters in the Americas if need be. China has been accumulating massive volumes of oil in storage in the last two years, apparently.

It should also be noted that the Ukraine crisis that started in 2014, which turned into a full-blown war in 2022, handed China an arguably massive geopolitical win, in the form of Russia becoming its most important ally. Between the roughly 7 mb/d in crude and products imports, it could import from Russia, its own domestic supply and ample storage levels, it could potentially withstand a prolonged shutdown of the Strait.

India recently agreed to reduce its Russian oil purchases, which it seems it is set to replace with Venezuelan oil imports. The problem it faces, in trying to replace imports from the ME, is that Russia is unlikely to prioritize India over China, in my opinion, given that it relented under US pressure and reduced its purchases of Russian oil. Other countries in Asia that are heavily dependent on oil from the ME region are likely to bid on any seaborne oil exports available.

The EU & UK will likely have to bid up the price of oil aggressively to capture some of the remaining sources of seaborne shipments that are not yet under contract. Some indications suggest that the world might have far less of a supply surplus cushion going into this crisis than it might be assumed. As I pointed out in a recent article, the EIA estimate on the global supply/demand balance, showing a 4 mb/d surplus, does not seem to add up, and it contradicts many of its other data points.

The surplus that the world did enjoy, which might be only half of the claimed volumes, seems to have mostly translated into China increasing its stockpile. Germany currently has about 100 days' worth of oil stockpiles, France has about 85 days worth, and Italy has about 75 days worth of stockpiles based on EIA estimates of OECD inventories.

Low volumes of domestic European supplies, combined with low oil inventories compared to many peers, as well as the possibility that the US might curb oil exports to protect its domestic economy from the international oil price spike, can translate into a widening gap between the US WTI oil price and the Brent spot price. Equinor, as a European oil producer, can see an outsized benefit relative to many industry peers

Investment implications.

It remains to be seen how long the Iran war lasts and how it will unfold. The key questions when it comes to energy markets remain how long Iran can sustain a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz and to what extent we may see the warring sides attack each other's energy facilities. There is a chance that extensive damage to the energy infrastructure in the region will cause supply disruptions well beyond the duration of the war. The duration of the war itself may be longer than expected, or it may suddenly and unexpectedly stop on a dime, catching markets by surprise.

For the duration of the war, Equinor is well-positioned to take advantage of rising oil & gas prices, given that Europe should see an outsized spike compared with the Americas, for instance.

As we can see, there is a clear divergence in Equinor's stock price relative to its peers, correlating with the start of this war. Suncor's (SU) oil sales are strongly tied to the North American market in terms of realized prices, as are Chevron's (CVX). Shell (SHEL) has broader exposure to the European energy market and a robust LNG portfolio. The problem is that part of its LNG export capacity is in Qatar. Equinor, with its robust Norwegian presence, can take advantage of European energy prices rising faster than they will in the Americas, while at the same time, it is less likely to suffer disruptions.

My short-term plan is to start taking profits as oil prices rise, selling stocks in other oil-producing companies in my portfolio first. Suncor is the largest stock position in my portfolio, and I have several other oil stock positions that I intend to take profits on, before I start taking profits on my Equinor stock position. I plan to start selling Equinor shares incrementally if its share price surpasses $40 during the current crisis. Assuming that its share price will decline once this war ends, I intend to start buying back shares incrementally once they decline to below $30.

As I stated in my prior article on Equinor, I see its long-term prospects as a prominent player in the European energy market as potentially very lucrative, given that Europe's energy markets seem to be in a constant state of near-crisis, with supply prospects always looking precarious. With this in mind, I intend to maintain a robust position in Equinor beyond the short-term volatility we see unfolding due to geopolitics. The generous dividend of 4.7% currently is an additional reason to stay long.

Risks to my thesis.

When it comes to trading geopolitical trends and their market effects, the impact on stocks is short term and most often the outcome can be unpredictable. By waiting for Equinor's share price to reach $40/share before I start selling to book profits, the risk is that I will miss out on booking profits if its share price never reaches that level.

Conversely, I may start selling Equinor shares in expectation that the war will end and there will be a pullback within a reasonable timeframe of weeks or months, but the conflict and resulting paralysis of ME energy exports could continue longer than anyone expects. Selling energy stocks into the current rally in the sector can leave investors on the sidelines of what could become a sustained long-term rally.

Conclusions.

Equinor stock currently makes up about 8% of my total portfolio. I built this position mostly last year on fundamentals, such as the low and declining European natural gas inventory levels. It goes without saying that the Iran war situation provides those of us with a sizable position in oil stocks to take profits. In my case, about a quarter of my stock portfolio is made up of oil & gas related stocks. If higher oil prices push these stocks higher, it makes sense to take some profits, but at the same time, I intend to maintain a sizable position in oil stocks, because the long term fundamentals suggest it is a solid long-term bet. Because no one knows what happens next with Iran, Russia or other potential flashpoints, I think it would be a mistake to unload one's entire oil position and then risk missing out as the price of oil might not come down after this rally, for the foreseable future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Superior Group Of Companies Remains Deeply Undervalued
data.description:
In November of last year, one company that I made the decision to revisit was Superior Group of Companies (SGC). Operationally, this is an interesting business. For those not familiar, it provides customers with customized merchandising products, promotional offerings, and branded uniforms. It has a special focus on the healthcare apparel market as well. But on top of this, the company operates multiple call centers under its Contact Centers segment, with locations throughout El Salvador, Belize, the Dominican Republic, and the US, dedicated to providing inbound and outbound support for customers on a contract basis.

I find this kind of business to be fascinating. And, over the last several months, I have been bullish about it. In fact, I reaffirmed it as a ‘buy’ candidate last November. Since that time, the stock has outperformed the market, rising 7.6% while the S&P 500 is up only 1%. The latest data provided by management came out on March 3rd, covering the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. To be clear, the company is a slow growth prospect at the moment. But even with that being the case, revenue and earnings per share came in above what analysts were hoping to see. What's more, management forecasted further growth in 2026. And when you add on top of this how cheap the stock is compared to other companies that have similarities to it, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘buy’ candidate is the right choice.

Not a call to downgrade yet

On March 3rd, the management team at Superior Group of Companies reported financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue amounted to $146.6 million. In addition to being above the $145.4 million the company reported a year earlier, it exceeded what analysts anticipated to the tune of $1.3 million. Bottom line results also surprised. Earnings per share, for instance, grew from $0.13 to $0.23, exceeding what analysts anticipated by $0.02. This translated to a rise in net profits from $2.1 million to $3.5 million. All other profitability metrics for the business improved as well. Operating cash flow, for instance, more than doubled from $8.9 million to $18.4 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a much smaller increase from $7 million to $7.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $7.3 million to $8.6 million.

When it comes to 2025 in its entirety, the picture for the business was a lot less impressive. As the chart above illustrates, revenue did increase. However, rising expenses pushed net profits and cash flows down across the board. This was disappointing to say the least. However, to really understand what is going on here, we would be best digging into each of its operating segments in turn. And perhaps the best way to start this would be through the largest of these segments, known as Branded Products.

This is the part of the company that sells customized merchandising solutions, promotional products, and branded uniforms, to customers that need them. Examples of customers here include players and retail, the hotel space, the food and service industry, entertainment, transportation, and even technology. For the year as a whole, revenue grew from $353.3 million to $361.1 million, with a decent chunk of that growth occurring in the final quarter. Management chalked this up to greater sales associated with its purchase of 3Point Brand Management, which is a branding firm that Superior Group of Companies purchased in December of last year. This cost shareholders only $6.4 million. But it brought in $11 million worth of revenue throughout 2025. What this means is that, absent this acquisition, actual revenue would have contracted.

That might help to explain why overall segment profits pulled back from $36.3 million in 2024 to $33.3 million last year. As disappointing as this was, during the final quarter of the year, profits amounted to $11 million. That was up nicely from the $8.9 million reported the previous year. This actually translated to margin improvement even though the company was negatively affected by tariffs. Management mentioned in their earnings call that they have been active in finding the lowest cost vendors globally without sacrificing quality. So selective shopping around certainly helped here. Management did acknowledge that they also look at hiking prices, though it seems as if they are cautious to do so. They aren't afraid, however, to turn away certain customers if those customers don't make sense for them from a revenue and profit perspective. So while the company is observant over the impact that price increases can have, they explicitly stressed that they are not doing this as a race to the bottom.

Unfortunately, other operating segments were weak during this time. The final quarter of 2025 saw revenue associated with its Healthcare Apparel segment contract to $28.8 million compared to the $30.3 million generated the previous year. And for the year as a whole, it dropped to $115.9 million compared to the $119.2 million reported the year before that. Management blamed this weakness on volume decreases within existing customer accounts. In essence, customers aren't buying as much as they were in the past. And it should come as no surprise that this would translate to lower profitability as the chart below illustrates.

We unfortunately saw a very similar situation when it came to the firm’s Contact Centers segment. Revenue in the final quarter was $21.7 million. That was down from $23.5 million reported a year earlier. And year over year for the fiscal year in its entirety, we saw a fall from $96.9 million to $92.5 million. This decline was blamed by management on macroeconomic headwinds that have pushed customers to downsize. These same issues have caused customer attrition that outpaced new customer acquisition activities. And as revenue drops, profitability pools back also.

These results are disappointing to say the least. However, there are reasons to be optimistic. For starters, Superior Group of Companies operates in markets that should continue to grow as time goes on. The Healthcare Apparel this segment operates in a $4.4 billion history, and that's focused only on the US. While overall revenue has been essentially flat over the last six years, management expects the size of the labor market here to grow by about 13% between 2020 and 2031. That means about 2.7 million new healthcare providers. And with the company already demonstrating an ability to grow scrub penetration rates from 70% at the start of this decade to 78% today, it is definitely well positioned for expansion. Similarly, the Branded Products space offers promise. The six year annualized revenue growth here was about 8%, and the total market size stands at $27 billion. Management hopes to benefit from this opportunity by growing its sales teams while also working to cut costs through supply chain adjustments. But both of these markets pale in comparison to the Contact Center industry. According to management, this market is worth about $121 billion in size. And that's confined only to the US. With an international presence, there is the prospect of additional expansion as time goes on.

The good news from all of this is that management is forecasting additional growth this year. They expect revenue to come in at between $572 million and $585 million for the year. That compares to the $566.2 million the company reported in 2025. With that, earnings per share should come in at between $0.54 and $0.66. At the midpoint, this would mean that profits of $9 million compared to the $7 million reported for 2025. If we assume that other profitability metrics should rise similarly, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $40.8 million and EBITDA of $33 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced at the moment. To be honest with you, these are very attractive multiples. I suppose that you could say that the price to earnings multiple puts the company more in the fair value range or even slightly overvalued range on an absolute basis. But when it comes to the other multiples, the stock is affordable. In the same chart, I stacked Superior Group of Companies up against five other similar enterprises. And what I found was that, across the board, it ended up being the cheapest of the group.

Takeaway

Right now, I believe that the upside that we have seen over the last few months when it comes to Superior Group of Companies is great. But I don't think the story ends here. Shares of the business are attractively priced, and the company looks set to continue growing in the long run even if we see some mixed results caused by broader economic concerns. It is likely that conditions will face some weakness as we near a recession. But, for those focused on the long haul, the stock is cheap enough to ride that wave.

data.title: Royce Micro-Cap Fund FY 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Performance

Royce Micro-Cap Fund (RYOTX) advanced 13.3 in 2025, lagging its benchmark, Russell Microcap Index, which was up 23.0% for the same period. Longer-term relative performance was better, however, as the Fund beat its benchmark for the 3-, 5-, 10-, and 25-year periods ended 12/31/25.

What Worked… and What Didn’t

Seven of the portfolio’s 10 equity sectors made a positive impact on calendar year performance, led by Industrials, Financials, and Information Technology. The largest negative impacts came from Consumer Discretionary, Health Care, and Real Estate. At the industry level, electronic equipment, instruments & components (Information Technology), banks (Financials), and aerospace & defense (Industrials) contributed most for the calendar year period, while professional services (Industrials), life sciences tools & services (Health Care), and software (Information Technology) were the largest detractors.

Our top contributor was nLIGHT (LASR), which designs, manufactures, and sells a range of high-power semiconductor and fiber lasers that are typically integrated into laser systems or tools built by its manufacturing customers. The company also provides components and integrated solutions to high-energy laser systems for directed energy and laser sensing systems used in a wide range of defense applications. nLIGHT differentiates its business by its vertical integration, domain knowledge, and manufacturing capabilities to combine dedicated resources and facilities with deep technical expertise to deliver cutting edge solutions, increasingly to government and defense organizations. Its shares have outperformed due to upward revisions to the outlook for its aerospace & defense customers. We remain constructive on the prospects for addressable market expanding product launches and a recovery in manufacturing-driven end-markets.

LightPath Technologies (LPTH) produces optical assemblies, modules, and integrated camera systems for the defense, public safety, and industrial end-markets. Its stock price rose as LightPath continued to deliver record backlog at record quarterly run-rate revenues amid a broad-based demand environment for components and systems, including those related to shipboard long-range surveillance, border security, and counter UAS (Unmanned Aircraft System). We remain constructive on LightPath’s evolution from an optical component manufacturer to vertically integrated provider of value-added infrared optics and camera systems, anchored by proprietary capabilities such as its germanium-free BlackDiamond infrared materials.

Astronics (ATRO) supplies flight critical electrical power, inflight entertainment and connectivity, lighting and safety, and test solutions to the aerospace, defense, and mass transit industries. The company holds a dominant market share in in-seat power systems for commercial aircraft. Accelerating activity levels related to improving aircraft production rates and ramping defense contracts drove its stock price performance in 2025. With more than two-thirds of revenue related to commercial aircraft, split close to equally between line fit and retrofits, Astronics will also benefit from airline fleet updates. We believe that sustained progress with process efficiencies and portfolio simplification will further benefit its through-cycle earnings power.

Sprott (SII) is a global alternative asset manager specializing in precious metals and real assets. The company operates a diversified platform of exchange-listed products, private equity funds, and lending strategies focused on gold, uranium, and energy transition metals. A combination of gold prices reaching record highs amid elevated geopolitical risk, central bank buying, and a weaker U.S. dollar drove the advance of its shares. Sprott’s suite of physical bullion trusts and energy transition ETFs saw substantial inflows, driving strong growth in assets under management and recurring fee revenue. The firm also benefited from robust performance in its private strategies, particularly in uranium and critical minerals lending. We remain constructive on continued operating leverage as the company’s global distribution scales, with new mandates secured across Europe and Asia. We also think that Sprott is well-positioned to compound earnings across commodity cycles.

CECO Environmental (CECO) offers highly engineered systems and process solutions that safeguard people, the environment, and industrial equipment across niche applications, including the industrial air treatment and management, water treatment, and energy transition end-markets. The company has installed more than $10 billion of equipment across 4,200 customers. Its stock outperformed as CECO continued to deliver record backlog at record quarterly run-rate revenues amid tariff and government shutdown uncertainty, owing to continued momentum in the construction of power generation, water treatment, and semiconductor infrastructure. Electrification, reshoring, and the buildout of advanced manufacturing are sustaining a broad-based demand environment.

The Fund’s top detracting position was outdoor products and accessories manufacturer American Outdoor Brands (AOUT) was negatively impacted by retail customer conservatism, as well as higher than expected tariff related costs. While management has been moving production out of China, many of its new manufacturing production partners, such as Vietnam, were also affected by the broad-based application of higher tariffs. Likewise, retailers in general continue to be cautious around inventory. That said, sell trough of the company’s products at retail remain robust, and we believe it positions the company for a more normalized retail/tariff environment over the more intermediate term.

Ichor Holdings (ICHR) is a key component supplier to the semiconductor industry. While from a revenue perspective the company has benefitted from a strong position with key customers, a nascent effort to drive margin improvements through a vertical integration strategy has struggled with poor execution, resulting in lower-than-expected margin and earnings performance, and a CEO transition. We still view the company as a key player in the industry and ultimately expect supply chain issues to be fixed and so added to our position on Ichor’s share price weakness.

PAR Technologies (PAR) provides Point of Sale ('POS') and other software products to the hospitality industry. The enterprise end-market among restaurant customers continues to move away from complex point solutions and/or costly internally developed technology, shifts that benefit PAR’s broad and integrated cloud platforms. The company remains in the midst of a multi-year transition toward a more focused, software-driven business model. And while PAR has successfully landed a number of large accounts such as Burger King, implementations, particularly in mid-sized clients, have been slower than anticipated as customers have paused investments due to concerns over economic growth and tariffs. We expect the slower pace of implementations to be short lived, however, and expect PAR to continue to win more than its fair share of new business.

QuinStreet (QNST) provides performance-based marketing activities across several verticals, including auto insurance and home Improvement. Auto insurance, the company’s largest vertical, has been a little weaker than anticipated as the insurers wait to see what inflationary impact tariffs may have on the cost of auto repairs. As the company uses its marketing expertise to build scale across a number of new verticals, we see a significant opportunity for operating leverage in the business model and thus have held our position.

Transcat (TRNS) is a global leader in test and measurement equipment as well as calibration services for a diverse set of industries, including life sciences, power generation, aerospace & defense, manufacturing, and food and beverage production. The company reported solid year-over-year revenue growth in November of 2025, but earnings were lower than expected, net income declined, and operating margins contracted, thanks to increased operating expenses tied to some recent acquisitions.

It was a very strong year for the asset class, as the Russell Microcap outperformed each of the major domestic indexes. Micro-caps were particularly strong off the early April bottom, rising 63.5% from 4/8/25-12/31/25. Both sector allocation and stock selection hurt, with the former detracting most. At the sector level, both stock selection and a much lower exposure to Health Care hurt relative results most and by a wide margin. The bulk of our underperformance, both in the sector and for the Fund as a whole, was attributable to our underweight in biotechnology and pharmaceuticals, two industries that tend not to fit our investment philosophy and which had significant moves following the April bottom. While we are always thoughtful and deliberate about sector weightings, we believe that staying true to our investment philosophy is critical to long-term term success, even when it causes relative performance issues in the short-term. Also hampering relative results were our higher weighting and stock selection in Consumer Discretionary and a lower weighting and stock selection in Energy. Conversely, stock selection and, to a lesser extent, our lower weighting in Financials, stock selection in Communication Services, and stock selection along with a lower weighting in Consumer Staples were each additive versus the Russell Microcap in 2025.

Current Positioning and Outlook

We believe that many of the trends that drove micro-cap performance in 2025 remain in place as we heading into 2026. First and foremost, underlying economic growth remains solid, while inflation continues to moderate. These trends were in place even before significant stimulus spending had fully impacted the economy. Both the Biden administration’s Infrastructure Investment Act and the Trump administration’s Big Beautiful Bill remain in the early stages of their respective rollouts. When combined with a generally accommodative Federal Reserve, the pieces appear to be in place for ongoing growth, which should benefit micro-caps. We are also beginning to see the impacts of a lighter regulatory touch and the burgeoning productivity effect of the significant investments in AI. We continue to invest with an eye toward economic growth, and the long-term impact of shrinking supply chains and reshoring. We also continue to look for opportunities in the supply chain associated with the AI buildout, as well as seeking companies that can more fully harness the potential productivity gains associated with the technology. More than ever before, smaller teams can have an outsized impact on U.S. businesses.

Of course, we are also mindful of potential pitfalls, the most important being the legitimate concerns of a bubble in AI spending. While we do not believe that one has developed yet, history shows that technology spending can reach dangerous excesses. And while inflation has been coming down, the meaningful fiscal spending and full impact of tariffs could reverse that trend and impact the pace of Fed easing. Likewise, while we believe we are through the worst of the psychological impact of tariffs, the current administration has behaved capriciously at times. Lastly, as the events in Venezuela and the earlier bombing of Iran’s nuclear facilities have shown, the administration appears to a have a robust foreign policy position. As these are very complex issues, they increase the risk of an outlier event.
